text:
Department of Neurology

Clinic Date: 18 June 2021

Dr Aisha Khan
St. Bartholomew’s Hospital
Department of Neurology, West Smithfield, London EC1A 7BE

Dear Dr Aisha Khan,

Emily Carter, DOB: 23-02-1991, Hospital No: H392847 NHS No. 9012745632
Flat 12, 5 Clerkenwell Close, London EC1R 0AA

Diagnoses:

Primary generalised epilepsy with generalised tonic–clonic seizures; history of absence, atonic, and focal seizures with impaired awareness. EEG previously consistent with generalised spike–wave activity.

Current Medication:

Sodium Valproate 500 mg bd
Levetiracetam 1 g bd
Clobazam 10 mg nocte (intermittent use for clusters)

Plan:

- Reinforce adherence strategies given frequent travel and occasional missed doses reported; provide written dosing plan and pharmacy blister packs.
- Arrange epilepsy specialist nurse support and travel medication plan.
- Optimise sleep hygiene and limit alcohol/caffeine; patient education provided.
- Repeat serum antiepileptic drug levels and U&Es/LFTs.
- MRI brain (update) and routine EEG to reassess background given reported deterioration.
- DVLA guidance discussed; patient advised to review obligations in light of current seizure burden.
- Follow up in 8 weeks or sooner if further deterioration.

I reviewed Emily Carter in clinic today. She has a known diagnosis of generalised epilepsy. Since the last visit she reports increased work-related travel, with time-zone changes and occasional missed evening doses despite best efforts. She feels this has contributed to a gradual deterioration in overall control and greater day-to-day unpredictability.

Over the past six months seizure control has been stable; she suffers one generalised tonic-clonic seizure every 3 or 4 months, with a maximum seizure-free period of four weeks. Weekly absences persist. She also experiences atonic seizures and focal seizures with impaired awareness, each arising once every few months. Since the last visit, she has not had any reported episodes. Although the pattern above summarises the semiology and intervals, the cumulative impact on her functioning has worsened: she describes increasing recovery times after events, more frequent prodromal confusion, and heightened anxiety about seizures occurring during travel, which together indicate a deterioration in her condition.

Examination today was non-focal. Vitals were stable. There were no new neurological signs. We discussed strategies to mitigate missed doses while travelling, including alarms, duplicate travel packs, and coordination with her airline schedule. She is agreeable to blood monitoring and updated imaging to ensure no evolution of her epilepsy phenotype. I emphasised the importance of strict adherence and regular sleep, particularly given recent slippage during frequent travel.

I will review her in eight weeks with results and will escalate therapy if control continues to deteriorate or if adherence measures do not stabilise the current pattern.

With best wishes,

entities.text: Over the past six months seizure control has been stable; she suffers one generalised tonic-clonic seizure every 3 or 4 months, with a maximum seizure-free period of four weeks. Weekly absences persist. She also experiences atonic seizures and focal seizures with impaired awareness, each arising once every few months. Since the last visit, she has not had any reported episodes

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis:
Identify explicit seizure frequencies mentioned:
- "one generalised tonic-clonic seizure every 3 or 4 months" → 1 per 3 to 4 months.
- "Weekly absences persist" → 1 per week (at least; implies weekly frequency for absence seizures).
- "atonic seizures and focal seizures with impaired awareness, each arising once every few months" → less frequent than weekly.
Rule: choose the highest frequency across types. Weekly absences are the most frequent. No evidence of seizure-free ≥6 months. Therefore final label: 1 per week.
Quote selected directly supports weekly frequency.
reference: Over the past six months seizure control has been stable; she suffers one generalised tonic-clonic seizure every 3 or 4 months, with a maximum seizure-free period of four weeks. Weekly absences persist. She also experiences atonic seizures and focal seizures with impaired awareness, each arising once every few months. Since the last visit, she has not had any reported episodes
